narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 71.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which led to the rejection of your application. Your profile includes being in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an LSAT score of 26, an undergraduate GPA of 3.40, upper-middle family income, identifying as a Black female planning to attend full-time. Compared to typical students, your LSAT is notably lower than the average of about 37, and your school deciles are at the very bottom versus the middle of the pack, while your GPA is a bit stronger than average and your family income aligns closely with norms. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your LSAT score, which pushed the prediction heavily toward expecting a bar failure. Next in influence came your first-year school decile rank, also pointing toward failure due to its low standing. Your third-year decile followed closely, reinforcing that concern in a similar way. Family income had a moderate pull in the same direction, as did your racial background, both contributing to the risk assessment relative to the dataset's majority patterns. Your undergraduate GPA nudged things slightly less toward failure, while your choice to go full-time had minimal impact. Interestingly, your gender as female was the one factor that gently pulled in the opposite direction, toward a higher chance of passing. Overall, these elements combined to outweigh the positives in the model's eyes. To improve your future chances, focus on bolstering test-taking skills through targeted LSAT retake prep or similar practice, as low scores like yours were the top predictor here—perhaps seek tutoring or study programs proven for bar success. Building stronger academic habits early, like those reflected in higher deciles, could also help if you're considering other paths or reapplications. Hang in there; many overcome these hurdles with dedicated preparation.
